perturbation.original_text: Company represents and warrants that it is in compliance, and covenants that it will continue to comply during the Term, with all applicable Laws, rules and regulations, including the federal anti-kickback statute (42 U.S.C. § 1320a-7b), the related safe harbor regulations, and the Limitation on Certain Physician Referrals, also referred to as the "Stark Law" (42 U.S.C. § 1395nn).
perturbation.changed_text: Company represents and warrants that it is in compliance, and covenants that it will continue to comply during the Term, with all applicable Laws, rules and regulations.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the specific mention of the federal anti-kickback statute and the Stark Law, the contract appears to weaken the compliance requirements related to healthcare regulations. This omission creates a legal risk because it fails to explicitly address critical laws designed to prevent fraud and abuse in healthcare, potentially leading to non-compliance issues.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification omits the explicit requirement for the company to comply with the Federal Anti-Kickback Statute (42 U.S.C. § 1320a-7b). This statute prohibits offering, paying, soliciting, or receiving remuneration to induce or reward referrals of items or services reimbursable by a federal healthcare program. By removing the specific mention of this law, the agreement potentially opens the door to practices that could violate the Anti-Kickback Statute.

perturbation.justification: The modified contract omits specific mention of the federal anti-kickback statute (42 U.S.C. § 1320a-7b), which is a critical law designed to prevent fraud and abuse in healthcare. The original text explicitly included this statute, ensuring compliance, while the changed text only refers to 'all applicable Laws, rules and regulations,' making the compliance requirement less explicit and potentially opening the door to practices that could violate the Anti-Kickback Statute. Scraped Law Snippet 1 directly references '42 USC 1320a-7b: Criminal penalties for acts involving Federal health care programs,' indicating the importance of this statute. Therefore, the omission creates a direct contradiction by weakening a key compliance obligation.

perturbation.original_text: Company represents and warrants that (1) it is not excluded from, and has not been convicted of any crime or engaged in any conduct that could result in exclusion from, participation in any state or federal healthcare program, as defined in 42 U.S.C. §1320a-7b(f), for the provision of items or services for which payment may be made by a federal healthcare program; (2) it has not contracted, and will not contract, with any employee, contractor, agent, or vendor to perform work under the Agreement who is excluded from participation in any state or federal healthcare program; and (3) it is not subject to a final adverse action, as defined in 42 U.S.C.§ 1320a-7a(e) and 42 U.S.C. § 1320a- 7a(g), and has no adverse action pending or threatened against it. Company shall notify Janssen of any final adverse action, discovery of contract with an excluded entity or individual, or exclusion within thirty (30) days of such action.
perturbation.changed_text: Company represents and warrants that it has not contracted, and will not contract, with any employee, contractor, agent, or vendor to perform work under the Agreement who is excluded from participation in any state or federal healthcare program; and Company shall notify Janssen of any discovery of contract with an excluded entity or individual within thirty (30) days of such action.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the first representation and warranty clause significantly weakens the assurance that the company is not already excluded from federal healthcare programs. Removing the 3rd clause, in turn, weakens the assurance of not being subject to adverse action. Omission of information relating to healthcare exclusions introduces significant compliance risks.
perturbation.law_explanation: The altered text removes the warranty that the company itself is not excluded from participation in federal healthcare programs. 42 U.S.C. §1320a-7 outlines the grounds for exclusion from these programs, and the original clause ensures compliance. By omitting this warranty, the contract fails to provide assurance that the company is eligible to participate in activities involving federal healthcare program funds, potentially violating this law.

Mandatory Exclusions Social Security Act 42 USC § Amendment 1128(a)(1) 1320a-7(a)(1) Conviction of program-related crimes. Minimum Period: 5 years 1128(a)(2) 1320a-7(a)(2) Conviction relating to patient abuse or neglect. Minimum Period: 5 years 1128(a)(3) 1320a-7(a)(3) Felony conviction relating to health care fraud. Minimum Period: 5 years 1128(a)(4) 1320a-7(a)(4) Felony conviction relating to controlled substance. Minimum Period: 5 years 1128(c)(3)(G)(i) 1320a-7(c)(3)(G)(i) Conviction of second mandatory exclusion offense. Minimum Period: 10 years 1128(c)(3)(G)(ii) 1320a-7(c)(3)(G)(ii) Conviction of third or more mandatory exclusion offenses. Permanent Exclusion Permissive Exclusions Social Security Act 42 USC § Amendment 1128(b)(1)(A) 1320a-7(b)(1)(A) Misdemeanor conviction relating to health care fraud. Baseline Period: 3 years 1128(b)(1)(B) 1320a-7(b)(1)(B) Conviction relating to fraud in non-health care programs. Minimum Period: 1 year 1128(b)(7) 1320a-7(b)(7) Fraud, kickbacks, and other prohibited act
perturbation.justification: The original contract contains a representation that the company is not excluded from participation in any state or federal healthcare program, as defined in 42 U.S.C. §1320a-7b(f). The changed text omits this representation. The Law Explanation says that this omission creates a failure to provide assurance that the company is eligible to participate in activities involving federal healthcare program funds, potentially violating this law. Scraped Law Snippet 1 references 42 U.S.C. § 1320a-7, which concerns the exclusion of certain individuals and entities from participation in Medicare and State health care programs. The omission of the representation weakens the assurance that the company complies with 42 U.S.C. § 1320a-7, which is a legally mandated consumer protection, creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Janssen represents and warrants that it is in compliance, and covenants that it will continue to comply during the Term, with all applicable Laws, rules and regulations, including the federal anti-kickback statute (42 U.S.C. § 1320a-7b), the related safe harbor regulations, and the Limitation on Certain Physician Referrals, also referred to as the "Stark Law" (42 U.S.C. § 1395nn).
perturbation.changed_text: Janssen represents and warrants that it is in compliance, and covenants that it will continue to comply during the Term, with all applicable Laws, rules and regulations.
perturbation.explanation: Similar to the previous change, removing the specific mention of the federal anti-kickback statute and Stark Law from Janssen's representations weakens compliance requirements and introduces legal risks. It fails to explicitly address critical laws designed to prevent fraud and abuse in healthcare, potentially leading to non-compliance issues.
perturbation.law_explanation: By omitting the explicit reference to the Stark Law (42 U.S.C. § 1395nn), the modified text weakens the assurance that Janssen will avoid physician self-referral practices that are prohibited under this law. The Stark Law restricts physicians from referring patients to entities with which they have a financial relationship for certain designated health services. Failing to explicitly acknowledge this law could create legal risks related to non-compliance.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: ve screening tests and vaccines at § 411.355(h) should apply to CPT code 90739 and are revising the Code List accordingly. The applicability of the exception for preventive screening tests and vaccines to CPT code 90739 is prospective only and effective on the date indicated on the UPDATED list of codes. In considering this comment, we also identified two CPT codes (90653 and 90658, both flu vaccines) that were inadvertently left off of the list of codes to which the exception for preventive screening tests and vaccines at § 411.355(h) should apply. Accordingly, we are adding these CPT codes to the list of codes to which the exception at § 411.355(h) applies, effective on the date indicated on the UPDATED list of codes. 2023 Annual Update to the Code List Below you will find the Code List that is effective January 1, 2023 and a description of the revisions effective for Calendar Year 2023. List of codes effective January 1, 2023, published December 1, 2022 Annual Update to the List of CPT/HCPCS Codes Effective January 1, 2023, published December 1, 2022 (PDF) The comment period ended December 30, 2022. We did not receive any comments related to the additions, deletions, and corrections to the codes on the Code List effective January 1, 2023. We received one (1) comment related to the supervision level required for specific services. We consider this comment to be outside the scope of the annual update. CMS does not respond to out of scope comments on the annual updates to the Code List. DHS Categories The DHS categories defined by the Code List are: clinical laboratory services; physical therapy services, occupational therapy services, outpatient speech-language pathology services; radiology and certain other imaging services; and radiation therapy services and supplies. The Code List also identifies those items and services that may qualify for either of the following two exceptions to the physician self-referral prohibitions: EPO and other dialysis-related drugs (42 CFR § 411.355(g)). Preventive screening tests and vaccines (42 CFR § 411.355(h)). NOTE: The following DHS categories are defined at 42 CFR §411.351 without reference to the Code List: durable medical equipment and supplies; parenteral and enteral nutrients, equipment and supplies; prosthetics, orthotics, and prosthetic devices and supplies; home health services; outpatient prescription drugs; and inpatient and outpatient hospital services. perturbation.justification: The omission of specific references to the federal anti-kickback statute and Stark Law weakens the original contract's compliance requirements. The Stark Law, as indicated in Scraped Law Snippet 1 (42 USC 1395nn), prohibits certain physician referrals, and by removing explicit mention of this law, the modified contract creates a legal risk of non-compliance. The omission undermines the assurance that Janssen will avoid physician self-referral practices, creating a direct conflict with the law's intent.
